segments.s487-g9: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. پیلان (elephants) is the subject; بدیدند is the simple past 'they saw'. ز + آتش is a poetic/archaic prepositional phrase 'from/of the fire' (here 'at the fire' or 'because of the fire'), and گریز is the noun 'flight' used elliptically to mean 'they fled' — the whole clause reads 'when the elephants saw (the) fire, (there was) flight'.
segments.s487-g10: برفتند is the past tense plural 'they went off/they departed' (here 'they fled away'). با + لشکر 'with the army' is a prepositional phrase marking accompaniment. از جای 'from (their) place' uses از + جای; تیز is an adjective/adverb 'swift' modifying how they left. Word order is verb then accompaniment then origin then manner ('they departed with the army from the place swiftly').
segments.s487-g11: ز لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'from the army' (poetic 'ز' = از). برآمد is the simple past 'arose/issued forth'. سراسر is an adverb/adjective 'throughout' and خروش 'roar/uproar' is the noun; together: 'a roar rose throughout the army.'
segments.s487-g12: به زخم آوریدند is an idiomatic causative phrasing: به + زخم (to wound) + آوریدند 'they brought/made' = 'they inflicted wounds' (transitive past). پیلان is the direct object 'the elephants'. به جوش is a circumstantial phrase 'in a surge/with boiling fury' describing the manner. Overall: 'they wounded the elephants in a furious surge.'
segments.s487-g13: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. خرطومهاشان is the plural possessed noun 'their trunks' (خرطوم + plural + possessive). بر آتش گرفت is a common verbal phrase meaning 'caught fire' ('بر' + noun + گرفت; گرفت here denotes 'took'/'caught'). The clause: 'when their trunks caught fire.'
segments.s487-g14: بماندند is the past plural 'they remained/they stood' (here 'they stood/paused'). زان is a contraction of از + آن 'from/at that' (here 'because of that' or 'thereat'). پیلبانان is the plural 'mahouts/elephant-drivers' (the agents who handle elephants). شگفت functions adjectivally/adverbially 'astonished/amazement' — together: 'those mahouts stood amazed at that.'
segments.s487-g15: همه لشکر هند is a noun phrase 'the whole army of India' (همه modifying لشکر, هند specifying origin). گشتند is the past plural of گشتن meaning here 'turned' or 'returned/turned back'. باز is an adverb 'again/back' reinforcing the sense of turning back ('turned back'). Word order: subject (whole phrase) then verb then adverb.
segments.s487-g16: همان is the demonstrative 'the same' pointing to the previously mentioned animals. ژنده is an attributive 'ragged' (literally 'rag'), modifying پیلان 'elephants'. گردن‌فراز is a compound adjective 'neck-uplifted' = 'proud' describing their bearing. The sequence is determiner + adjective + noun + adjective ('the same ragged elephants, proud-necked').
segments.s487-g17: سکندر is the subject (Alexander). پس is an adverb placed after the name meaning “then/thereupon.” لشکر بدگمان is a noun phrase (لشکر + postpositive adjective بدگمان = “the wary/suspicious army”) placed before the verb to indicate the force accompanying Alexander (classical Persian often juxtaposes such companions without a preposition).
segments.s487-g18: همی is the archaic/prose particle used with verbs to mark ongoing or emphatic action (here “was/continued to”). تاخت is the main verb meaning “galloped/charged.” بر سان is a two-word simile marker (بر + سان) = “upon/like” and باددمان is a poetic simile-image “wind-bearing/gust-like”; together they form the simile “charged like a gust of wind.”
segments.s487-g19: چنین تا is a correlative phrase meaning “so (much) that/so that,” introducing a result clause. هوا نیلگون شد is a verbal clause with هوا (the atmosphere/sky) as subject, نیلگون (indigo/blue) as predicate adjective and شد the stative verb “became.” به رنگ is a prepositional phrase meaning “in the color/hue of,” specifying the quality.
segments.s487-g20: سپه را uses را as the definite/object/topic marker (here topicalizing the army). نماند is the perfective/sta tive negative “remained/not left” (i.e. ‘there was no longer’). آن زمان is a time adverbial “at that time/then,” and جای جنگ is a noun phrase “place/room for battle,” the complement of نماند: there remained no place for fighting.
segments.s487-g21: جهانجوی is an epithet/noun (“world‑seeker,” here Alexander) as subject. با رومیان is a comitative prepositional phrase “with the Romans,” and همگروه is an adjective/noun meaning “fellow‑group/ally” that further characterizes the association — together they indicate Alexander was allied or accompanied by the Romans.
segments.s487-g22: فرود آمد is a verbal compound meaning “alighted/descended/came down” (فرود here is the noun/verb stem and آمد the auxiliary/main verb). اندر میان دو کوه is a locative phrase: اندر = “in/within,” میان = “between,” دو کوه = “two mountains,” giving the place of descent.
segments.s487-g23: طلایه فرستاد is a verb phrase ‘sent a vanguard/advance guard’ (طلایه = vanguard, فرستاد = sent/dispatch). هر سو به راه is an adverbial phrase: هر سو = “every direction/side,” and به راه = “on the road/way” or “along the route,” specifying where the vanguard was sent.
segments.s487-g24: همی داشت is the progressive construction (همی + داشت) meaning “kept/was keeping.” لشکر is the theme/subject marked earlier; ز دشمن نگاه is a short genitive/agentive phrase where ز (poetic) = “concerning/from” and نگاه = “watch/lookout,” so the whole means the army kept watch regarding/against the enemy.
segments.s487-g25: چو functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. پیدا شد is a verbal phrase meaning 'appeared/was seen' where پیدا is adjectival/participial 'visible' and شد is the past/becoming verb. آن + شوشه تاج شید is a stacked noun phrase (آن = 'that/the'), with poetic apposition/compounding (no ezāfe) joining شوشه ('plume') to تاج ('crown') and شید as a following epithet; the whole phrase is the subject of پیدا شد.
segments.s487-g26: جهان شد is a simple past/resultative clause 'the world became'. بسان introduces a simile 'like/as', followed by the noun phrase بلور سپید where بلور ('crystal') is modified by the adjective سپید ('white/bright') placed after it in normal Persian order; the whole means 'became like a white crystal.'
segments.s487-g27: برآمد خروش is verb + noun 'a cry arose' (برآمد = 'arose/issued forth', خروش = 'cry/roar'). از بر گاودم is a prepositional phrase 'from/on the cow's tail' (از = 'from', بر = 'on/upon', گاودم = 'cow's tail'), attached to the verb to indicate source or location of the sound.
segments.s487-g28: This segment strings together nominal phrases that describe sounds or objects: دم نای = 'the breath (دم) of the reed/flute (نای)' with an implicit genitive relation; سرغین و رویینه خم are coordinated nouns joined by و ('and'), where رویینه qualifies خم (an adjectival/attributive relation). Poetic word order and omission of ezāfe allow these nouns to stand side by side.
segments.s487-g29: سپه با سپه places two noun phrases in opposition 'army with/against army' (با = 'with/against' here indicating confrontation). جنگ is the object 'war/battle' and برساختند is the past transitive verb 'they raised/they made', so the clause literally reads 'army with army made war' — a compact, poetic SOV-like ordering.
segments.s487-g30: سنانها is the subject 'the spears' (plural). به ... اندر افراختند is the verb phrase 'they raised up into ...', where به + اندر together mark direction/goal ('towards/into') and افراختند is the past tense 'they raised/erected'. ابر is the locative goal 'the clouds' — 'they raised their spears into the clouds.'
segments.s487-g31: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'. بیامد is the simple past 'came'. میان دو صف is a prepositional phrase 'between two ranks/lines' (میان = 'between', دو صف = 'two battle-lines'), completing the motion/location of the verb.
segments.s487-g32: یکی is an indefinite pronoun 'one/a certain (man)'. تیغ رومی is a noun phrase 'Roman sword' (تیغ = 'sword', رومی = adjectival origin 'Roman'). گرفته is the past participle/verb 'having taken/holding' used here as the main verb, and به کف is a locative phrase 'in the palm/hand' (به + کف) showing how the sword is held.
segments.s487-g1: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'). Subject اسکندر precedes the past verb آمد ('came/drew'), then the prepositional phrase به نزدیک ('to/near') marks the approach; فور is an adverb ('at once') modifying the arrival. Word order (چو + subj. + verb + locative + adverb) is typical of classical Persian temporals.
segments.s487-g2: بدید is a simple past ('saw'). آن سپه is a demonstrative noun phrase ('that host'), then این سپه را repeats/clarifies the object with the proximal demonstrative and the accusative/topic marker را; ز دور ('from afar') is a final adverbial. The duplication آن … این … را is a poetic device for emphasis/contrast.
segments.s487-g3: خروش آمد is an impersonal/periphrastic expression ('a cry arose' — آمد here = 'happened, came about'). و links clauses. گرد رزم here means 'the dust of battle' (گرد = dust) and از دو روی is an adverbial ('from two sides'). The noun phrase precedes the next verb as is common in poetic inversion.
segments.s487-g4: برفتند is a past plural verb ('they advanced/proceeded') and here takes گردان ('troop/squadron') as its subject after the verb (verb–subject inversion is frequent in poetry). پرخاشجوی is an adjective 'aggressive' describing گردان.
segments.s487-g5: This lists targets of the action with repeated preposition به ('to/onto'): به اسپ و به نفط = 'to the horses and to the oil.' آتش اندر زدند is a poetic collocation meaning 'they struck/laid fire among' (i.e. they set fire). اندر ('in/among') places the fire into those targets; زدند is the past plural verb.
segments.s487-g6: همه لشکر is the subject ('the whole army/host'). فور is an adverb ('at once/immediately') and برهم زدند is a compound verb meaning 'put into disorder/overturned' (literally 'struck into confusion'). The verb is past plural to agree with the collective subject.
segments.s487-g7: از آتش is an agent/source phrase ('from the fire'). برافروخت is a past verb meaning 'kindled/flared up' and takes نفط سیاه ('black oil') as its (grammatical) object/affected thing; the order places the source first for emphasis. برافروخت is a classical/perfective form often used poetically for 'ignited'.
segments.s487-g8: بجنبید here is plural past/preterite meaning 'were set in motion / were roused' (a reflexive/causative sense). ازان ('from that, because of that') links the previous event to the result: کاهنین بد سپاه = 'the ill (bad) host of those priests' — کاهنین (priests) qualified by بد (bad/evil), with سپاه (host/army) as the noun. Word order groups modifiers after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s487-g41: Structure: Subject + indirect object + verb + vocative. سکندر is the subject; بدو is the enclitic/prepositional pronoun 'to him' placed before the verb; گفت is the past tense main verb 'said'; کای نامدار is a vocative phrase ('O renowned one') addressing the addressee. Note کای is an archaic/vocative particle.
segments.s487-g42: Word order: numeral + noun (دو لشکر) marks the subject; شکسته (past participle) + شد (auxiliary) form a resultative/passive ("were broken/defeated"); از کارزار is a prepositional phrase 'from the battle' giving cause/place. The participle + شد construction produces a passive/result state typical of older Persian.
segments.s487-g43: همی is an adverb conveying continuous or repeated action ('kept/continually'); دام و دد 'traps and foes/beasts' is the compound subject; مغز مردم is the object in the genitive order ('the people's brains'); خورد is the past tense 'ate/devoured'. The line uses compressed epic word order common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s487-g44: همی again gives ongoing action. نعل اسپ is a possessive compound 'the steed's horseshoe' (noun + noun), استخوان is the object 'bones', and بسپرد is the past form of سپردن used here in a resultive/causative sense ('sent/consigned' → poetically 'crushed/left as bone'). The verb appears at the end as is normal in Persian.
segments.s487-g45: دو + مردیم marks 'we two' (مردیم is an older/poetic first‑person plural copular form meaning 'we were/we are men'); هر دو emphasises 'both'; دلیر و جوان are coordinated adjectives 'brave and young' describing the two. Note مردیم is archaic here compared with modern Persian.
segments.s487-g46: سخن گوی is an adjectival phrase meaning 'eloquent' or 'a speaker' (literally 'speech-speaking'); و با مغز دو پهلوان links another quality: 'and with brain (intellect) two champions' — i.e. the two are also champions in intellect. The adjective phrases follow the subject as is common in poetry.
segments.s487-g47: دلیران لشکر is a noun phrase 'the brave ones of the host/army' (qualifier + noun). همه ('all') quantifies the subject; کشته‌اند is intended as the perfect/passive 'have been killed' (here reflected in the epic's terse past form). The provided sense list for کشته‌اند does not match the verbal passive meaning, hence -1.
segments.s487-g48: وگر is a conditional connector 'but if' introducing the alternative; زنده 'alive' is the predicate adjective; از رزم برگشته‌اند is a perfect construction 'have returned from the battle' with the verbal compound برگشته‌اند at the end. The clause offers the contrasting possibility that survivors, if any, came back from combat.
segments.s487-g97: خروشی (a noun formed from خروش) is the subject 'a cry/clamour'. برآمد is the past tense 3rd‑singular verb 'arose/issued forth'. ز (poetic for از) marks source 'from the host' (ز لشکر). به زار is an adverbial phrase: به + زار ('in a wailing/lamenting manner'); here به works to form an adverbial modifier with زار.
segments.s487-g98: فرو is an adverb/prefix meaning 'downward' modifying ریختند, which is the past plural 'they fell/they poured/they collapsed' (here 'fell down'). آلت کارزار is a genitive/compound: آلت 'instrument(s)' + کارزار 'of battle' = 'instruments of battle'. The verb agrees with a plural understood subject (the instruments or the things of battle).
segments.s487-g99: پر از درد is a fixed adjectival phrase 'full of pain' (پر 'full' + از + noun). نزدیک شد(ند) is a verbal phrase 'to draw near/approach'—here شدند is the past plural 'they became/they came (near)' with نزدیک as its complement. قیصر is the object/goal of نزدیکی 'to Caesar'.
segments.s487-g100: پر از ناله 'full of lamentation' parallels the previous structure (پر + از + noun). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. خاک بر سر شدن is an idiomatic light‑verb construction: خاک 'dust' + بر 'on' + سر 'head' + شدن 'become' → 'to throw dust on one’s head / to lament'; شدند is past plural 'they did so / became thus'.
segments.s487-g101: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'. سلیح گوان is a compound/phrase meaning 'the weapon‑bearing ones' or 'armed men' (سلیح 'weapon' + گوان used to indicate 'those of/like'), functioning as an appositive or descriptive phrase. بازداد here is a verbal past form meaning 'gave back / responded' — contextually 'he answered/returned (a cry)'.
segments.s487-g102: به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'well/very strongly' (به + خوبی). ز هرگونه is a prepositional phrase 'in/from every kind/way' modifying the manner of the following verb. آواز داد is a light‑verb sequence 'gave voice / cried out' (آواز 'call/voice' + داد 'gave') meaning 'he cried/shouted'. The whole string describes how he answered (i.e. by uttering various cries loudly).
segments.s487-g103: چنین گفت is the reporting clause 'thus he said' (چنین 'thus' + گفت 'said' in past 3rd singular). کز is the poetic relative/conjunction 'that' introducing the reported clause. هند مردی بمرد is the content clause 'a man from India died' — مردی is the indefinite 'a man' and بمرد here functions as the past/declarative of 'to die' in this older style (introducing the fact that he has died).
segments.s487-g104: شما را uses را as a topical/definite object marker attached to شما 'you'. به غم دل is a dative/target phrase 'to (the) grief of the heart' (به + غم + دل). نباید is the impersonal negative modal 'should/must not'. سپرد is the infinitival/participial verb 'to entrust/hand over' (here in the sense 'to give over/consign'), so نباید سپرد = 'you must not give over/entrust (yourselves)'. Overall: 'You must not give your hearts to grief.'
segments.s487-g81: برآمد is the past verb 'arose/issued forth' placed at the start (verb-initial order, common in poetry). دم بوق literally 'breath/blast of the horn' is a noun+noun construction (no ezafe written) that functions as a single noun phrase; و links this phrase to آواس کوس, where آواس (archaic spelling of آواز) + کوس = 'sound of the drum' (again the possessor follows the possessed without an explicit ezafe). The subject performing 'برآمد' is the combined sound(s) implied by these noun phrases.
segments.s487-g82: This is two short adjectival predicates in sequence. زمین آهنین شد uses زمین (subject) + adjective آهنین + past verb شد 'became' (simple past). The following هوا آبنوس omits an explicit verb by parallelism to شد: noun هوا followed by predicate adjective آبنوس ('ebony/very dark'), so the meaning is 'the earth became iron, (and) the air (became) ebony.' The compact order and lack of conjunction are typical of classical poetic compression.
segments.s487-g83: بران هم is an adverbial phrase: بران 'thereupon/on that' and هم 'also/too' introduce what follows. نشان هندوان رزمجوی is a nominal chain 'the standard/sign of the Indian warriors' where نشان is the head noun and هندوان رزمجوی is a genitive-like modifier (poetic word order without an explicit ezafe). The whole segment names the object/action introduced by 'بران هم'.
segments.s487-g84: به تنگی is an adverbial phrase built with به + abstract noun تنگی 'narrowness/close quarters' meaning 'in tight formation' or 'closely'. The clause به روی اندر آورده روی literally uses آورده (past participle / past form 'brought') with اندر (archaic/inward preposition) and a doubled روی 'face' for emphasis: 'they brought (their) faces inward/toward (one another)'. The subject is omitted (understood 'they/warriors'), and the repetition of روی is a poetic device emphasizing faces coming close together.
segments.s487-g85: خروش آمد is a simple past clause 'a cry/roar came'. از روم is a prepositional phrase 'from Rum' (Byzantium/Europe). کای دوستان is a vocative inserted after the clause: کای is an archaic vocative marker 'O' and دوستان here is vocative 'friends' (direct address). The vocative placement after the main clause is a rhetorical, poetic move.
segments.s487-g86: This is a compact noun phrase in apposition or exclamation: سر مایه مرز هندوستان. سر here functions as an intensifier/head 'chief' (or 'head'), مایه 'resource/cause', and مرز هندوستان 'the border/frontier of India' (noun + modifier, again with no explicit ezafe). Together they form 'the chief resource/force of the Indian frontier' — a terse exclamatory description.
segments.s487-g87: سر فور هندی به خاک اندرست places the noun سر 'head' first, followed by the adverb فور 'immediately', the modifier هندی 'Indian', and the locative phrase به خاک 'on the earth'; اندرست is an older-form present copula 'is within/on'. The clause thus reads literally 'the Indian's head immediately is on the ground' (i.e. 'the head of the Indian was immediately on the ground'). The verb is stative/present in form but used narratively.
segments.s487-g88: تن پیلوارش به چاک اندرست is another nominal sentence: تن 'body' modified by پیلوارش (an adjectival/qualifying form meaning 'along its length/continuous') and the predicate به چاک اندرست 'is in gashes/was split open'. The construction omits an overt copula (uses اندرست) and packs the description tightly: 'his body, all along, was cleft/ruptured.'
segments.s487-g73: سکندر (proper name) is the sentence subject; چو باد is a simile meaning “like the wind” (چو = like/as + باد = wind). اندر آمد is the past tense verb ‘came in’ (اندر is an older/poetic locative), and ز گرد is a prepositional phrase ‘from the dust/around’ (ز = from; گرد here = dust), giving manner/place of entry.
segments.s487-g74: بزد is a simple past verb ‘he struck/struck down’; Persian often places the verb before its object in poetry. تیغ تیزی is a noun + attributive: تیغ ‘sword’ qualified by تیزی ‘sharpness/keenness’ (adjective-like after the head). بران is a demonstrative/prepositional element ‘upon that’ linking the action to the following noun phrase, and شیر مرد is a compound image ‘lion-man’ = a heroic/lion‑hearted man (object of the strike).
segments.s487-g75: ببرید is the past tense of بریدن ‘to cut’ (here ‘he cut off’); the provided lemma senses did not include ‘cut’, so the lexical fit is irregular. پی بر بر is a compact poetic sequence: پی ‘after/in succession’ + repeated بر, giving an immediate/forceful or successive stroke sense; و links to the next phrase. گردن‌ش is ‘his neck’ (گردن = neck; ـش = enclitic third‑person possessive).
segments.s487-g76: ز بالا به خاک indicates motion/location ‘from above to the earth’ (ز = from; بالا = above; به خاک = to the soil). اندر آمد is a poetic intransitive ‘came/was brought into’ and is followed by تنش ‘his body’ (تن = body, with enclitic ـش = his) placed at the end for emphasis — common in Persian verse where the noun with possessive enclitic can appear post-verb.
segments.s487-g77: سر لشکر روم is a genitive noun phrase ‘the heads of the Roman army’ (سر = head; لشکر روم = Roman host). شد here functions as a past/resultative ‘became / were’ meaning the heads ended up ‘to the sky’ (به آسمان), i.e. were thrown up or lifted — a concise poetic expression.
segments.s487-g78: برفتند is a past plural verb ‘they went/they departed’ (subject omitted but understood). It is followed by the moved noun phrase گردان لشکر دمان, in which گردان ‘troop/unit’ modifies لشکر ‘army/host’ and دمان is a poetic noun (here likely ‘the ranks/skirts/edge’); word order (verb before the noun phrase) is acceptable in verse and emphasizes the action of departure.
segments.s487-g79: یکی introduces an indefinite/existential ‘there was one / a certain’; کوس بودش = ‘there was a drum’ (کوس = war-drum; بودش = بود + enclitic = ‘it was’). ز چرم هژبر is a genitive/partitive phrase ‘made from the leather of Hejbar’ (ز = from; هژبر here is a proper name), describing the drum's material.
segments.s487-g80: که introduces a relative clause ‘whose/which’; آواز او is ‘its sound’ (آواز = voice/sound; او = its/he/she). برگذشتی is a poetic past form meaning ‘passed over/pierced through’ (here ‘passed beyond’ the clouds); the exact morphological option given in the lemma list did not match this third‑person reading, so the sense is treated as the poetic intransitive ‘passed over’. ز ابر ‘from the cloud(s)’ indicates the sound went beyond/through the clouds.
segments.s487-g89: شما را is the personal pronoun 'you' plus the object/definite marker را (marks you as the affected party). کنون is a temporal adverb 'now' placed after the marked object. از پی is a prepositional phrase 'after/in pursuit of' (از + پی) modifying the copula clause, and کیست is the fused interrogative 'کی' + copula 'است' ('who is'), so the whole chunk asks 'who now is following you in battle' (جنگ here names the sphere: fight/battle).
segments.s487-g90: چنین is a demonstrative adverb 'such/so' qualifying the following noun. زخم شمشیر is a noun phrase 'wound(s) of the sword' (زخم governed by شمشیر). و connects the two coordinated items; چندین درنگ is another noun phrase 'many a pause/hesitation' where چندین is an adjective 'several/many a' and درنگ means 'pause/delay'. The phrase enumerates the state (wounds and hesitations) that characterize the one mentioned earlier.
segments.s487-g91: سکندر is the subject proper name 'Alexander'. شما را again uses را to mark 'you' as object/affected. چنان شد is a resultive expression: شد (become/happen) with چنان 'so' meaning 'did this to you / things became thus toward you'. که introduces the result clause ('so that'), and فور is an adverb 'immediately' pointing to a prompt reaction.
segments.s487-g92: ازو جست is a simple past/action clause: ازو 'from him' + جست (past of جستن here 'to spring/leap') meaning 'he leapt from him' or 'sprang out of him' (an abrupt motion). باید is the modal 'must/ought'; همی is an archaic/poetic particle here intensifying or adverbially accompanying the following obligation. رزم و سور is a coordinated nominal phrase 'battle and feast' (رزم = combat, سور = banquet), expressing what 'must' be had.
segments.s487-g93: برفتند is the past plural verb 'they departed/they went forth'. گردان is the noun 'troop/unit', and هندوستان is the place-name 'India' used as the destination/identification of those units; word order is verb–object–adjunct (verb first) which is common in epic narration.
segments.s487-g94: به here functions as an instrumental/temporal marker 'at/with' (by means of); آواز is 'shout/call' so به آواز = 'at the shout'. گشتند is plural past of گشتن used as 'became' (they turned into/grew so), and همداستان is an adjective 'like-minded/united in purpose' describing their condition after the shout.
segments.s487-g95: تن is 'body'. فور here is a proper name (the same For/Hindi in the narrative) rather than the adverbial sense, so it functions as a name modifying or following تن (name-tokenization in the source is ambiguous). دیدند is the past plural 'they saw'. پر + noun is a construction meaning 'full of X' (پر خون و خاک = 'full of blood and dust'), with پر placed before the nouns it governs.
segments.s487-g96: بر ... و تنش is an elliptical/poetic phrasing meaning 'his breast and his body' (بر here functions as part of the compound referring to the upper torso). ش is the enclitic possessive 'his' attached to تن (تنش = 'his body'). کرده is the past participle/compound of کردن used to mean 'made' or 'rendered' (here 'made torn'), and به شمشیر چاک is an instrumental phrase 'by the sword, (a) gash' with چاک as the resulting 'gash/rend'. The clause describes the state caused by the sword.
segments.s487-g113: چنین است is a formulaic clause meaning “thus is/so it is.” رسم is the head noun “custom.” سرای سپنج is a possessive phrase (poetic/archaic word order without an overt ezafe) meaning “the house/household of Sepanj”; the provided senses for سرای and سپنج do not include this proper‑noun/house reading, so those items are treated as a name/possessor here.
segments.s487-g114: بخواهد is third‑person volitive/present of خواستن used impersonally: “(they) want/wish.” که introduces the subordinate clause. مانی is the proper name Mani (direct object/subject of the wished action). بدو = به + او (to him/unto him). در + به + رنج is a compact, poetic prepositional phrase meaning “into/into the state of suffering” (در = in/into, به = towards/into used here adverbially, رنج = suffering).
segments.s487-g115: بخور is an imperative here (literal “eat/take” used idiomatically as “take”/“seize”). هرچ داری is a free relative: “whatever you have” (هرچ = whatever, داری = you have). منه is مِنْهُ (from him), and بازپس (in this collocation) means “take back / reclaim”; the verb and following words form an imperative instruction to recover/keep what one possesses.
segments.s487-g116: تو is the vocative/subject “you.” رنجی (رنج + ـی) = “your suffering” / “a suffering of yours.” چرا = “why.” ماند is past/stative “remain” (here meaning “be left/linger”). باید = “must/ought” (modal). به کس = “to/with anyone” (به used as a prepositional marker, کس = anyone/person). Together: rhetorical question about why the addressee’s suffering should remain with anyone.
segments.s487-g117: همی بود is a past habitual/continuous construction: همی intensifies/marks continuity and بود is past “was.” بر تخت قیصر = “on Caesar’s throne.” دو ماه is a temporal adverbial “for two months,” placed after the verb in the flexible, poetic word order.
segments.s487-g118: ببخشید here is past third‑person singular of بخشیدن used in the benefactive sense “gave/bestowed.” گنجش (گنج) = “treasure/wealth” (the direct object), همه = “all,” and بر سپاه = “upon/to the army” (بر used as a directional/benefactive preposition).
segments.s487-g119: یکی = “a certain / one” (indefinite introducer). با گهر = “with a jewel” (با marks accompaniment/possession here). بود is past “was.” نامش = نام + enclitic ش = “his name” (the possessive clitic). سورگ here is a personal name (proper noun), not covered by the provided senses.
segments.s487-g120: ز (archaic/poetic preposition) = “from.” هندوستان = “India.” پهلوانی is a substantive here meaning “champion/heroic prowess” (used as a noun describing him: a champion). سترگ is an attributive adjective meaning “great/mighty” in context (the supplied senses do not reflect this usage, so it is treated as a lexical item with no matching provided sense).
segments.s487-g65: سکندر چو دید = a temporal clause: 'چو' (archaic/poetic) = 'when', followed by the past-tense verb 'دید' 'he saw'. 'آن تن پیل مست' is a noun phrase in apposition: 'آن' = 'that', 'تن' = 'body/person', 'پیل' = 'elephant' used as a descriptive epithet ("elephant-bodied" = very large), and 'مست' = 'intoxicated' or figuratively 'stunned/inebriated'. Together: 'When Alexander saw that elephant-bodied, drunken person.'
segments.s487-g66: This verbless descriptive clause modifies the preceding noun phrase: 'یکی' = 'one (of them / a certain one)'; 'کوه' = 'mountain' used to indicate great size; 'زیر اژدهایی' literally 'under/like a dragon' here read as a genitival/qualifying phrase giving the dragon's size or relation ('a dragon as large as a mountain'); 'به دست' = a locative/instrumental phrase 'in the (his) hand'. Altogether: 'one who held a dragon as large as a mountain in his hand.'
segments.s487-g67: بآورد ازو ماند اندر شگفت: the verb 'بآورد' (past) here means 'caused/produced' (it brought about in him), 'ازو' = 'from him' (i.e., affecting him), 'ماند' = 'remained', and 'اندر شگفت' = 'in astonishment/wonder'. So: 'It left him remaining in astonishment.'
segments.s487-g68: غمی شد دل از جان خود برگرفت: 'غمی شد' = 'sorrow/grief arose' (شد = became/occurred). 'دل' = 'heart', 'از جان خود برگرفت' is a compact poetic clause: literally 'took away from his life his heart' or 'his heart withdrew from his life' — 'از' = 'from', 'جان' = 'life', 'خود' = 'his own', and 'برگرفت' = 'took away/removed'. The phrase conveys that grief so seized him his heart was taken away or abandoned life (poetic hyperbole).
segments.s487-g69: همی گشت با او به آوردگاه: 'همی' is the epic/progressive particle often meaning 'kept/was', 'گشت' here = 'moved/turned/went' (imperfective/habitual sense in poetry), 'با او' = 'with him', and 'به آوردگاه' = 'to the battlefield' (به = to + آوردگاه = battlefield).
segments.s487-g70: خروشی برآمد ز پشت سپاه: 'خروشی' = 'a roar/clamor', 'برآمد' = 'arose/issued forth', 'ز' (poetic form of از) = 'from', 'پشت سپاه' = 'the rear of the army' ('پشت' = 'behind/rear', 'سپاه' = 'army').
segments.s487-g71: دل فور پر درد شد زان خروش: 'دل' = 'heart', 'فور' = 'immediately', 'پر درد شد' = 'became full of pain' ('پر' = 'full (of)', 'درد' = 'sorrow/pain', 'شد' = 'became'), and 'زان خروش' = 'from that roar' ('زان' = 'from that', 'خروش' = 'roar'). The clause says his heart was instantly filled with grief by that noise.
segments.s487-g72: بران سو کشیدش دل و چشم و گوش: 'بران سو' = 'toward that direction/thereupon', 'کشیدش' = 'drew/pulled it' (the verb takes the object clitic '-ش' referring back to the body parts), and the list 'دل و چشم و گوش' = 'heart and eye and ear' — meaning the cry drew his heart, his gaze, and his attention toward that side. Word order places the verb before the objects (poetic style).
segments.s487-g49: چرا begins a rhetorical question 'why'. بهر لشکر همه is a prepositional/possessive phrase 'for the army / on behalf of the army, all (of them)'. کشتن است uses the verbal noun (infinitive) کشتن + copula است: the infinitive functions as the predicate ('the killing is' → 'they are to be killed' / 'must be killed'), so the clause asks why the rule/expectation is that everyone should be killed. Word order is typical older Persian: purpose phrase + object + verbal noun + copula.
segments.s487-g50: وگر is a conditional 'but/and if'. زنده از رزم برگشتن است again uses an infinitive (برگشتن) with است: literally 'a returning alive from the battle is (possible)'; here it conveys 'if anyone returns alive from battle'. The clause contrasts with the previous one: 'and if (one) returns alive from the fight?'.
segments.s487-g51: میان را ببندیم is a jussive/subjunctive expression ('let us / we should close/block the middle/space'); را marks میان as the definite object. و connects the coordinated verb phrase; جنگ آوریم is a light-verb construction (noun + آوردن) meaning 'to wage/bring about war' — here 'let us make war' or 'let us go to battle'.
segments.s487-g52: چو باید که introduces a purpose/necessity clause: چو 'when/since', باید 'it is necessary / we must', and که here is the complementizer 'that'. کشور به چنگ آوریم is idiomatic: به + چنگ (claw/grasp) + آوردن 'to bring' = 'to seize/capture the country'. آوریم is the subjunctive 'let us/that we bring/seize'.
segments.s487-g53: ز ما 'from among us / of us' sets the domain. هرک is an indefinite relative 'whoever' and او گشت is 'he became' (گشت here = 'becomes/become' in the general/fateful sense). پیروز بخت is a noun phrase 'victorious fate/fortune' — word order is poetic (adjective/epithet and noun). The whole means 'whoever among us becomes favored by victorious fortune'.
segments.s487-g54: بدو ماند is an impersonal/active construction 'remains to him' (بدو = 'to him', ماند = 'remains/shall remain'). این لشکر و تاج و تخت lists the possessions 'this army and crown and throne' that will be left to him. The verb ماند carries the resultative sense 'will be left / will belong'.
segments.s487-g55: ز رومی سخنها 'words/reports from the Romans' is the object/phrase placed before the verb in poetic order. چو + بشنید is a temporal clause 'when he heard'; بشنید is past-tense 'he heard'. فور is an adverb 'immediately' modifying the following action.
segments.s487-g56: خریدار شد is a metaphorical/nominal expression: خریدار literally 'buyer' but here 'one who takes/accepts'; شد is past 'became'. رزم او را — رزم 'the fight/battle', او را marks it as his/that fight — is the object taken up. به سور 'with/for a feast' indicates the consideration or occasion (he accepts/takes the fight for a banquet). Overall: 'he at once became the taker of that fight, for a feast.'
segments.s487-g121: سر تخت شاهی is a prepositional/locative phrase meaning “on the royal throne” with سر used poetically as ‘top/atop’; بدو داد is the past tense clause ‘gave to him’ (داد = gave) where بدو = 'to him'. و گفت is the connective + past verb ‘and said’, introducing reported speech that follows.
segments.s487-g122: که introduces the content clause (‘that/that is’). The clause دینار هرگز مکن در نهفت is an imperative prohibition: دینار (direct object) + هرگز (adverb ‘never’) + مکن (archaic negative imperative ‘do not do’). در نهفت is a prepositional phrase ‘in/into concealment’ (literally ‘in hiddenness’), giving the action to be avoided: do not hide the dinar.
segments.s487-g123: ببخش و بخور are two second‑person singular imperatives joined by و: ‘give (away) and eat’. هرچ آید فراز is a relative/indefinite phrase ‘whatever comes (آید) from on high/forth (فراز)’, with the object/quantity omitted and understood: ‘give away and consume whatever comes (to you).’
segments.s487-g124: بدین here functions as ‘to/with this’ (classical form of به + این) and introduces the noun phrase تاج و تخت سپنجی ‘this crown and throne that are spongy/unstable’. مناز is used as a prohibitory/advisory predicate meaning ‘do not make your abode / do not take as a dwelling’ (poetic/archaic usage) — i.e. don’t settle or be attached to this throne.
segments.s487-g125: که introduces an explanatory clause (‘for/that’). گاهی Sکندر بود is ‘sometimes Alexander was’ (بود = was, past copula). گاه فور repeats the adverbial idea with گاه ‘at times’ and فور ‘a rush/sudden surge’ — contrasting the occasional occupants or natures of the throne.
segments.s487-g126: گهی is another adverb ‘sometimes’. The coordinate pairs درد و خشمست and گه کام و سور list alternately negative and positive states: درد (affliction/suffering) and خشمست (wrath/anger), and then گه (at times) کام (desire) و سور (feast/revelry). The nouns are predicates describing the changing nature of rule or the throne.
segments.s487-g127: درم داد is ‘he gave dirhams’ (داد = gave). The conjunction و links دینار to the monetary giving; لشکرش را with the object marker را marks the definite direct object ‘his army’ as the recipient/beneficiary of that provision: ‘he gave dirhams and dinars to his army.’
segments.s487-g128: بیاراست is a past tense verb meaning ‘he adorned/equipped/arrayed’. It takes گردان کشورش را as its definite object (را marking the object): گردان here means ‘troops/divisions’ and کشورش is ‘his country’ (کشور + possessive ش). The phrase means he organized/arrayed the military divisions of his realm.
segments.s487-g105: نوزاش is نوزا (newborn) plus the enclitic -ش (‘his’), so ‘his newborn/infant’. کنون (now) is a temporal adverb. من is the explicit subject. به افزون کردن is a periphrastic way to say ‘to add/increase’ (به + افزون used as a notion of ‚more/increase‘) and کنم is the 1st-person present-subjunctive used here with an intentional/future sense: “I will add/increase.” Word order places the possessed noun and time before the subject and the verbal phrase.
segments.s487-g106: بکوشم is the 1st-person present-subjunctive of کوشیدن (‘I will try / I shall endeavour’). که introduces the subordinate clause of purpose/result (‘that / so that / in order that’). غم is the direct object 'sorrow'; نیز after the noun means ‘also’. بیرون + کنم is a causative/periphrastic expression: ‘to make/go out’ → ‘to drive out / expel’. The clause thus reads ‘I will try that I also drive out sorrow’ → ‘I will try to drive sorrow out as well.’
segments.s487-g107: ببخشم is the 1st-person present-subjunctive of بخشیدن here meaning ‘I will give / bestow’. شما را marks the (emphatic/definite) recipient ‘you’. همه گنج اوی = ‘all the treasure of him’ (اوی is an archaic possessive = ‘his’). The verb precedes the recipient phrase in this poetic word order but the meaning is ‘I will give you all his treasure.’
segments.s487-g108: حرامست here functions as a predicative phrase ‘is an insult / is shameful’ (predicate + -ست ‘is’). بر لشکرم is a prepositional phrase ‘to/upon my army’ (لشکری + -م ‘my’). رنج اوی = ‘his suffering’. The word order places the predicate first then the prepositional phrase and the noun phrase giving the sense ‘His suffering is an affront to my army.’ Note the archaic possessive اوی.
segments.s487-g109: همه هندوان را is the definite direct object ‘all the Indians’ (را marking definiteness/emphasis). توانگر is an adjective/noun ‘rich / prosperous’; توانگر کنم literally ‘I will make (them) rich’ — the adjective acts as object complement and کنم carries the causative/forte meaning ‘I will cause/make’. The omitted pronoun object of کنم is the previously-mentioned هندوان.
segments.s487-g110: بکوشم again is ‘I will try’. که introduces purpose/result (‘that / so that’). با + تخت و افسر is an instrumental/benefactive list ‘with throne and helmet’ (تخت = throne, افسر here = helmet/crown). کنم is the same 1st-person verbal form ‘I will make / provide’. The object is omitted (understood as the Indians) so the clause means ‘I will try to provide them with throne and helmet’ (i.e. seat them on thrones and crown/arm them).
segments.s487-g111: وزان is a connective adverb ‘and from that / thence’. جایگه (place/position) with شد ‘became/took’ means ‘he took the position/seat’. بر تخت = ‘upon the throne’. فور is an adverb ‘immediately’. The phrase thus reads ‘and from that place he immediately took the throne.’ Note classical/poetic inversion of elements.
segments.s487-g112: بران = بر + آن ‘on that / upon that’, introducing the first occasion: جشن ماتم ‘a festival/ceremony of mourning’. برین = بر + این (here written برین) ‘on this’, introducing the contrasting occasion: جشن سور ‘a festival/feast of rejoicing’. The verbs are omitted (typical of classical Persian); the clauses mean ‘on that, a mourning festival; on this, a rejoicing feast.’
segments.s487-g33: سواری (object) precedes the past verb فرستاد, a common word order in classical Persian narrative: '[he] sent a horseman.' نزدیک modifies the act of sending (indicating proximity or 'nearby'), and فور is an adverb 'immediately' (poetic/archaic) intensifying the verb. The implied subject is the previously named actor (Sikandar).
segments.s487-g34: که introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that / in order that'). او را uses the object marker را after the pronoun (him). بخواند is subjunctive/purpose-form of خواندن used here in the sense 'to call/summon' (classical use of خواندن). بگوید follows as another verb in the same purpose clause meaning 'to say/tell' (the lemma senses supplied don't list the verbal reading, so treat it as the verb 'say'). ز دور is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from afar' and modifies بگوید.
segments.s487-g35: که here introduces a subordinate clause ('that/when'). آمد is simple past 'came.' سکندر is the subject; به پیش سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'to the front / into the presence of the army' (به = toward/into, پیش = 'in the presence of', سپاه = army).
segments.s487-g36: به دیدار is a prepositional nominal phrase 'toward a meeting.' جوید is the present/habitual form 'seeks' (classical narrative uses present to indicate immediate action), and همی is an archaic/poetic continuous particle reinforcing ongoing action. با تو راه ('a way with you') completes the object of جویدن: 'to look for a way to meet with you' (راه is the thing sought).
segments.s487-g37: سخن (a noun 'speech/words') is the theme; گوید here functions as a verb 'he says/speaks' (the provided lemma senses do not list the verbal sense, so treated as verbal). The conjunction و links actions; گفت is another verbal form 'he said' (past), and تو بشنود is purpose/resultal: بشنود is subjunctive 'may you hear' after گفت indicating the intended receiver should hear. This clause uses repetition (present/past verbs) as a poetic device.
segments.s487-g38: اگر introduces a conditional 'if.' دادگویی here means 'a plea/advocacy (for justice)' rather than an abstract 'justice.' The clitic/prefix ب before دان is the classical prefix be- attached to the following element, and دان as segmented in the data isn't serving its listed senses here (together ب + دان read as 'to that' or anaphoric 'toward that'). بگرود is a classical subjunctive/optative form (ب‑ + گروید stem) meaning 'he may turn/ incline (to it)' — i.e. 'he will turn to it' in sense and mood typical of epic Persian conditional results.
segments.s487-g39: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' بشنید is past 'he heard.' The short form ز + و (زو) is the poetic 'from him' (ز = from, و here functioning as an enclitic/continuative 'then/so'). فور is the adverb 'immediately.' هندی is a noun/adjective 'the Indian' (subject of the following verb), and برفت is past 'went away/left' (بر‑ prefix + رفت). Together: 'When he heard from him, the Indian at once departed.'
segments.s487-g40: به پیش سپاه آمد is a simple clause 'he came to the front of the army' (به = to/toward, پیش = in the presence of, سپاه = host, آمد = came). از introduces the origin 'from,' قلب is 'heart/core,' and تفت here functions poetically as 'scorching / heat / the blazing centre' (used substantively); together 'from the heart of the blaze/heat' describes where he emerged. Note: تفت is a poetic noun here derived from a root meaning 'to scorch.'
segments.s487-g57: تن خویش را دید: a typical Persian SOV pattern where the definite object 'تن خویش را' ('his own body') appears before the past-tense verb 'دید' ('he saw'). 'خوی' plus the enclitic 'ش' forms the possessive 'خویش' ('his own'); 'را' is the definite/object marker. 'با زور شیر' is a prepositional phrase headed by 'با' ('with') linking 'زور' ('strength') to its possessor 'شیر' ('lion') (i.e. 'with the lion's strength').
segments.s487-g58: یکی باره چون اژدهای دلیر: 'یکی باره' is an adverbial compound meaning 'all at once' or 'suddenly' (here formed from 'یکی' + 'باره'). 'چون' is the comparative/simile preposition 'like', followed by 'اژدهای دلیر' ('a brave/valiant dragon'); the whole phrase is a simile modifying the preceding clause.
segments.s487-g59: سکندر سواری بسان قلم: a nominal phrase identifying Alexander as a rider. Persian often strings nouns without an explicit copula. 'بسان' is the comparative 'like/as', and 'قلم' here is used metaphorically ('hand/style') — i.e. 'in the manner/style (of a master)'. The phrase therefore links Alexander to a particular manner of riding.
segments.s487-g60: سلیحی سبک بادپایی دژم: a string of adjectival/attributive words describing the rider. 'سلیحی' (from سلیح) functions as 'armed' or 'bearing weapons'; 'سبک' = 'light' (here 'light in weight/movement'); 'بادپایی' is a compound meaning 'wind-footed' or 'fleet-footed' (swift of foot); 'دژم' is an adjectival descriptor 'grim/sullen'. Persian allows multiple adjectives to follow the head noun or nominal phrase.
segments.s487-g61: بدوگفت کاینست آیین و راه: 'بدوگفت' is a past-tense compound meaning 'he said to him' (the dative element is fused). 'کاینست' = 'که این است' (archaic/poetic contraction) meaning 'this is' or 'such is'. 'آیین و راه' are the two nouns being defined — 'custom/practice and way/method'. The clause introduces reported speech or a statement of principle.
segments.s487-g62: بگردیم یک با دگر بی‌سپاه: 'بگردیم' here is a hortative/subjunctive first-person-plural form meaning 'let us turn/go/return' (the 'ب' prefix with the present-subjunctive stem gives a jussive sense). 'یک با دگر' literally 'one with the other' = 'the two of us together'. 'بی‌سپاه' is 'without an army' (بی- prefix = without + سپاه 'army'), completing the proposal.
segments.s487-g63: دو خنجر گرفتند هر دو به کف: 'دو خنجر' is the object 'two daggers' placed before the past plural verb 'گرفتند' ('they seized/took'). 'هر دو' ('both/either') emphasizes that each man is involved, and 'به کف' ('into the palm/in hand') uses the preposition 'به' + 'کف' to indicate that they took the daggers into their hands.
segments.s487-g64: بگشتند چندان میان دو صف: 'بگشتند' (past plural) here means 'they turned/they moved about'. 'چندان' is an adverb meaning 'so much' or 'to that degree' (intensifying the movement), and 'میان دو صف' is the prepositional phrase 'between the two ranks/lines' (میان = between; صف = battle-line/formation). The clause describes their movement amid the opposing lines.

sentences.s487-s5: When the elephants saw the fire, they fled swiftly with the army from their place.
sentences.s487-s6: A great roar rose from the army; they wounded the elephants in a surge of fury.
sentences.s487-s7: When their trunks caught fire, those elephant-drivers stood there amazed.
sentences.s487-s8: All the Indian host turned back; the same ragged elephants remained proud-necked.
sentences.s487-s9: Then Alexander, with his wary army, rushed on like a gust of wind.
sentences.s487-s10: So much so that the air turned indigo in hue; the army had no place left for battle at that time.
sentences.s487-s11: The world‑seeker, accompanied by the Romans, descended into the space between two mountains.
sentences.s487-s12: He sent out vanguards in every direction along the road and kept his army on the watch for the enemy.
sentences.s487-s13: When that plume — the crown of Shīd — appeared, the world became like a white crystal.
sentences.s487-s14: A cry arose from the ox's tail; the breath of the reed, filth and the iron-bent bow.
sentences.s487-s15: Army with army raised war; they lifted their spears up into the clouds.
sentences.s487-s16: Alexander came between the two ranks; one man had a Roman sword taken into his hand.
sentences.s487-s1: When Alexander drew near, he immediately saw that host — this host from afar.
sentences.s487-s2: A cry arose, and the dust of battle rose from both sides; aggressive squadrons advanced.
sentences.s487-s3: They set fire to horses and to the oil; the whole host at once was thrown into disorder.
sentences.s487-s4: From the fire the black oil flared up; at that the ill‑omened host of those priests was roused.
sentences.s487-s21: Alexander said to him, "O renowned one, two armies were broken in the battle."
sentences.s487-s22: Continuously traps and beasts devoured the people's brains, and the steed's hoof kept crushing them into bone.
sentences.s487-s23: We two were both brave and young, eloquent in speech and two champions in wit.
sentences.s487-s24: The brave men of the army have all been killed; but if any are alive, they have returned from the battle.
sentences.s487-s49: A clamour rose from the host in lamentation; the instruments of battle fell down.
sentences.s487-s50: Full of pain, they drew near to Caesar; full of lamentation, they threw dust upon their heads.
sentences.s487-s51: Alexander, like the armed men, answered; he uttered a loud cry in every manner.
sentences.s487-s52: Thus he said that a man from India had died: 'You must not give your hearts over to grief.'
sentences.s487-s41: A blast of the horn and the drum's sound rose; the earth became iron, the air ebony.
sentences.s487-s42: Thereupon, too, the Indian battle-standard of the warriors — they drew up in close order, bringing their faces together.
sentences.s487-s43: A cry rose from Rum: 'O friends — the chief resource of the Indian frontier!'
sentences.s487-s44: The Indian's head lay at once upon the ground; his body, along its length, was rent to gashes.
sentences.s487-s37: Alexander came in like the wind from the dust, and he struck with a sharp sword at that lion-hearted man.
sentences.s487-s38: He cut him off—head and neck—and his body fell from above down to the earth.
sentences.s487-s39: The heads of the Roman host were lifted to the sky; the army's troop units departed.
sentences.s487-s40: There was a drum of Hejbar’s leather, whose sound passed beyond the clouds.
sentences.s487-s45: Who now is it that follows you in battle, with such sword-wounds and many a pause?
sentences.s487-s46: Alexander treated you so that at once he leapt from him; one must indeed have both battle and feast.
sentences.s487-s47: The Indian regiments departed; at a shout they became of one mind.
sentences.s487-s48: They saw For's body full of blood and dust; his breast and body rent by the sword.
sentences.s487-s57: Thus is the custom of the house of Sepanj: they want that Mani be put into suffering.
sentences.s487-s58: Take whatever you have and seize it back from him; why should your suffering remain with anyone?
sentences.s487-s59: He was on Caesar’s throne for two months and bestowed all his treasure on the army.
sentences.s487-s60: There was a certain man with a jewel; his name was Surag, a mighty champion from India.
sentences.s487-s33: When Alexander saw that elephant-bodied, drunken man—one who held a dragon as large as a mountain in his hand.
sentences.s487-s34: It left him standing in astonishment; grief came, and his heart withdrew from his life.
sentences.s487-s35: He kept moving with him toward the battlefield; a roar rose from the rear of the army.
sentences.s487-s36: His heart at once became full of pain from that roar; that cry drew his heart, eyes, and ears toward that side.
sentences.s487-s25: Why must the whole army be put to death — and what if (any) return alive from the battle?
sentences.s487-s26: Let us close the center and make battle, since we must seize the country.
sentences.s487-s27: If any one of us becomes (by) victorious fate, this army and the crown and the throne will remain to him.
sentences.s487-s28: When he heard the words from the Romans, he immediately became willing to take up the fight for a feast.
sentences.s487-s61: He set him on the royal throne and said, “Never hide (away) dinars.”
sentences.s487-s62: Give away and eat whatever comes from above; do not make this spongy crown and throne your dwelling.
sentences.s487-s63: For sometimes Alexander would be (on it), sometimes a sudden surge; sometimes it is pain and wrath, and sometimes desire and revelry.
sentences.s487-s64: He gave dirhams and dinars, outfitted his army, and arrayed the divisions of his realm.
sentences.s487-s53: Now I will add more for his newborn; I will try also to drive sorrow out.
sentences.s487-s54: I will give you all his treasure; his suffering is an affront to my army.
sentences.s487-s55: I will make all the Indians rich; I will try to seat them on thrones and give them helmets.
sentences.s487-s56: And from that place he immediately took the throne; on that [occasion] a festival of mourning, on this [occasion] a feast of rejoicing.
sentences.s487-s17: He immediately sent a horseman nearby, so that he might call him and tell (him) from afar.
sentences.s487-s18: That Alexander came forward before the host; he seeks a meeting and is looking for a way to come to you.
sentences.s487-s19: He utters speech and spoke so that you might hear; if you plead for justice he will turn to that.
sentences.s487-s20: When he heard it from him, the Indian immediately departed; he came before the host out of the very heart of the blaze.
sentences.s487-s29: He saw his own body, with the strength of a lion, all at once like a brave dragon.
sentences.s487-s30: Alexander rode — a horseman in the manner of a practiced hand; armed, light, fleet-footed, and grim.
sentences.s487-s31: He said to him, 'Such is the custom and the way; let the two of us go together without an army.'
sentences.s487-s32: The two seized daggers, each taking one in his hand; they turned about amid the two battle-lines.
